programs.name: Community Medicaid
programs.tagline: Home care services for aging parents who need daily help but want to stay out of nursing homes

localEntities.name: Local county DSS offices; MLTC plans vary by region.

programs.intro:
If your parent needs help with daily activities like bathing, dressing, or medication management, Community Medicaid can cover home health aides, personal care, nursing visits, and adult day care to keep them safely at home. This program is designed for New Yorkers 65+ who would otherwise need nursing home care but prefer to age in place.

Your parent must have income below $1,800/month and assets under $33,038 (2026 limits), plus need assistance with at least 3 activities of daily living. Because Community Medicaid covers the full cost of assessed care needs, families often save $3,000-$8,000 per month compared to paying privately for similar home care services.

The program works through Managed Long Term Care plans that coordinate all services under one team, so your parent won't need to manage multiple providers or appointments.

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must meet Nursing Facility Level of Care (clinical assessment showing need for daily help with at least 3 activities like bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, or mobility)
otherRequirements: New York resident
Legal U.S. resident (citizenship not required)
Intent to remain indefinitely if non-citizen

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online through NY State of Health or call 855-355-5777 for screening — processing typically takes several weeks to months.

steps.title: Get eligibility screening
steps.description: Call 855-355-5777 or visit nyhealth.gov to check if your parent meets income and asset limits

steps.title: Schedule clinical assessment
steps.description: If financially eligible, your parent will need a Nursing Facility Level of Care assessment to determine if they need help with 3+ daily activities

steps.title: Submit application with documents
steps.description: Apply online at NY State of Health, by phone, or at your local county Department of Social Services office with all required documentation

steps.title: Choose managed care plan
steps.description: Once approved, select a Managed Long Term Care plan in your area that will coordinate your parent's home care services

applicationGuide.processingTime: Several weeks to months depending on completeness of application
applicationGuide.waitlist: Possible for certain services or managed care plans
applicationGuide.tip: Gather 3 months of bank statements and recent medical records before applying — incomplete documentation is the main cause of delays.

contentSections.heading: Income Limits by Household Size
contentSections.footnote: 2026 limits (138% Federal Poverty Level with $20 disregard)

tiers.name: Managed Long Term Care (MLTC)
tiers.coverage: All home care services coordinated by one plan, requires long-term care need over 120 days

tiers.name: Community First Choice Option (CFCO)
tiers.coverage: Personal care and home health services, requires help with 3 of 5 basic daily activities

categories.name: Identity & Residency
items: Utility bills or lease showing New York address
Proof of legal U.S. residency status

categories.name: Financial
items: 3 months of bank statements for all accounts

categories.name: Medical
items: Recent doctor notes about daily care needs
Documentation of help needed with bathing, dressing, eating, etc.

contentSections.text: If your parent's income is even $1 over the limit, they won't qualify unless you set up a Pooled Income Trust first. Don't transfer assets to qualify — there's a 60-month look-back period that can create penalties.
Apply in the county where your parent lives. Each county has its own Department of Social Services office, and managed care plan options vary by region.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she gets Community Medicaid?
faqs.answer: Yes, your parent can keep their primary residence. The home is an exempt asset and won't count toward the $33,038 asset limit, regardless of value.

faqs.question: What if Dad's Social Security is $1,850 — just over the income limit?
faqs.answer: He won't qualify unless you set up a Pooled Income Trust to bring his countable income below $1,800/month. The trust pays for his care expenses with the excess income.

faqs.question: How many hours of home care will Mom get?
faqs.answer: There's no set number of hours. After approval, a care coordinator assesses your parent's specific needs and authorizes services accordingly — this could range from a few hours daily to around-the-clock care.

faqs.question: Can we choose Mom's home health aide?
faqs.answer: Your parent will work with their assigned Managed Long Term Care plan to select from approved home care agencies. You can request specific aides, but availability depends on the agency's staff.

faqs.question: What happens if Dad needs more care than Community Medicaid covers?
faqs.answer: The program is designed to provide nursing home level care at home. If his needs exceed what can safely be provided at home, he may need to transition to Nursing Home Medicaid.

faqs.question: Does Community Medicaid cover adult day programs?
faqs.answer: Yes, adult day care is a covered service if it's part of your parent's approved care plan. The managed care plan coordinates this along with other home-based services.

programs.name: New York State Home and Community-Based Services (HCBS) Waiver Programs
Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE)
programs.tagline: If your parent needs nursing home level care but wants to stay home, PACE provides all medical care, transportation, and daily support through one coordinated team.

localEntities.name: ElderONE (Rochester Regional Health): Monroe, Ontario, Wayne Counties
ArchCare Senior Life PACE: Bronx (900 Intervale Avenue, Bronx, NY 10459), Westchester

programs.intro:
If your parent is 55+ and has been certified as needing nursing home level care, PACE can keep them at home while providing comprehensive medical and social services. Because PACE covers everything from doctor visits to transportation to home health aides under one program, your parent won't need to coordinate between separate providers — one interdisciplinary team manages all their care.

PACE includes primary care by geriatricians, nursing, physical therapy, transportation to appointments, prescriptions delivered to your door, dental and vision care, hearing aids, meals at day centers, and 24/7 care coordination. Most participants are eligible for both Medicare and Medicaid, but private pay options exist. Your parent can disenroll at any time.

PACE is only available in specific areas of New York — currently Monroe, Ontario, and Wayne Counties (through ElderONE) and Bronx and Westchester (through ArchCare Senior Life).

summary: Able to live safely at home with support
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must be certified by New York State as needing nursing home level care (daily help with bathing, dressing, medication management, or other activities of daily living) and require more than 120 days of community-based long-term care services.
otherRequirements: Live in the service area of a PACE organization
Able to live safely in the community with PACE assistance

applicationGuide.summary: Contact your local PACE provider directly — there's no central application process.

steps.title: Check if PACE operates in your area
steps.description: PACE is only available in Monroe, Ontario, Wayne Counties (ElderONE) or Bronx and Westchester (ArchCare Senior Life).

steps.title: Contact your local PACE provider
steps.description: Call ElderONE for western NY or ArchCare for Bronx/Westchester areas directly.

steps.title: Complete nursing home level assessment
steps.description: New York State will assess whether your parent meets the clinical requirements for nursing home level care.

steps.title: Verify long-term care needs
steps.description: Document that your parent requires more than 120 days of community-based long-term care services.

applicationGuide.tip: Call the PACE provider first to confirm your parent's address is in their service area before starting the assessment process.

offices.name: ArchCare Senior Life PACE
offices.address: 900 Intervale Avenue, Bronx, NY 10459 - Serves Bronx and Westchester

categories.name: Identity
items: Birth certificate or other proof of age

categories.name: Residence
items: Proof of address in PACE service area
Lease, mortgage statement, or utility bill

categories.name: Medical
items: Recent medical records showing need for daily assistance

contentSections.text: PACE is not available statewide. You must live in Monroe, Ontario, Wayne Counties (ElderONE coverage) or Bronx and Westchester (ArchCare coverage). Check your exact address with the provider first.
Once enrolled in PACE, it becomes your parent's sole source of Medicare and Medicaid benefits. They cannot use other Medicare or Medicaid plans while in PACE, but can disenroll at any time.

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their regular doctor if they join PACE?
faqs.answer: No. PACE provides all medical care through their own interdisciplinary team of geriatricians, nurses, and specialists. Your parent's current doctors will no longer be covered, but the PACE team coordinates all care under one roof.

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is too high for Medicaid?
faqs.answer: PACE accepts private pay participants who meet all other requirements but don't qualify for Medicaid. Most participants are dually eligible for Medicare and Medicaid, but private pay options exist.

faqs.question: Can my parent leave PACE if they don't like it?
faqs.answer: Yes. Your parent can disenroll from PACE at any time and return to regular Medicare and Medicaid coverage.

faqs.question: Does PACE cover transportation to medical appointments?
faqs.answer: Yes. PACE includes transportation to all medical appointments, the PACE day center, and other program activities as part of their comprehensive services.

faqs.question: What happens if we move to a different county in New York?
faqs.answer: If you move outside your PACE provider's service area, your parent will need to disenroll from PACE. PACE is only available in specific counties and you must live within the service area boundaries.

faqs.question: How does PACE work with my parent's existing Medicare coverage?
faqs.answer: PACE replaces your parent's Medicare coverage entirely. All Medicare benefits are provided through the PACE program instead of through traditional Medicare or Medicare Advantage plans.

programs.name: Medicare Savings Program (MSP) - QMB, SLMB, QI
programs.tagline: Get help paying Medicare premiums, deductibles, and copays if your parent's income is below $1,715/month.

localEntities.name: Local Department of Social Services (DSS) in each county.

programs.intro:
If your parent struggles to afford Medicare premiums and out-of-pocket costs, New York's Medicare Savings Program can eliminate these expenses entirely. The program has three tiers based on income: QMB covers all Medicare costs including deductibles and copays, while SLMB and QI cover Medicare Part B premiums only.

New York uses more generous income limits than many states. Your parent may qualify with income up to $1,715/month for a single person ($2,320/month for a couple) depending on the tier. Because this program works directly with Medicare, your parent keeps their existing doctors and coverage — they just stop paying certain Medicare costs.

The QI tier operates on a first-come, first-served basis each year and can fill up. If your parent might qualify, apply early in the year to secure a spot.
programs.savingsSource: Medicare Part B premium ($185/month) plus potential deductibles and copays

otherRequirements: Must be eligible for Medicare Part A
U.S. citizen or qualified immigrant
New York resident

applicationGuide.summary: Call your local Department of Social Services or use form BRC-1071 (E) — processing takes up to 45 days.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Collect Medicare card, recent bank statements, income proof (Social Security award letter or pay stubs), and ID documents.

steps.title: Contact your local DSS office
steps.description: Call your county's Department of Social Services or NYC's HRA Medicaid Helpline at 888-692-6116 to request form BRC-1071 (E).

steps.title: Submit application
steps.description: Complete the Medicare Savings Program form and submit by phone, mail, or in-person to your local office.

steps.title: Wait for processing
steps.description: QMB applications take up to 45 days. SLMB and QI can be processed with retroactive coverage up to 3 months.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Up to 45 days for QMB; SLMB/QI may have retroactive coverage up to 3 months
applicationGuide.waitlist: QI-1 operates first-come, first-served annually (January-December) and may fill up
applicationGuide.tip: Apply for MSP-only using the short form if your parent's income is above regular Medicaid limits but below MSP thresholds.

tiers.name: QMB (Qualified Medicare Beneficiary)
tiers.coverage: Medicare Part A premiums, Part B premiums, deductibles, coinsurance, and copays

tiers.name: QI-1 (Qualifying Individual)
tiers.coverage: Medicare Part B premiums only, auto-qualifies for Extra Help prescription drug coverage

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
excluded: Burial plots and prepaid funeral arrangements
Life insurance policies (up to certain limits)

contentSections.text: QI-1 slots are limited and allocated first-come, first-served each January. If your parent might qualify, apply early in the year to avoid missing out.
New York offers more generous income limits than federal minimums. Your parent may qualify even if they wouldn't in other states — QMB goes up to 138% of federal poverty level instead of 100%.

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their current Medicare doctors if they get MSP?
faqs.answer: Yes. MSP works with your parent's existing Medicare coverage and doesn't change their doctors or health plans. The program simply pays the Medicare costs your parent currently pays out-of-pocket.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent's income is just over the QMB limit but under SLMB?
faqs.answer: They can still qualify for SLMB, which covers Medicare Part B premiums (about $185/month in 2026). While SLMB doesn't cover deductibles and copays like QMB does, it still provides meaningful savings on monthly premiums.

faqs.question: Does my parent's house count toward the asset limit?
faqs.answer: No. Your parent's home is exempt regardless of value, as is one car of any value, burial plots, and reasonable amounts of life insurance and personal belongings.

faqs.question: How long does MSP coverage last once approved?
faqs.answer: QMB and SLMB coverage continues with annual renewals. QI-1 coverage is limited to one year and you must reapply each January on a first-come, first-served basis.

faqs.question: Can my parent have both MSP and regular Medicaid?
faqs.answer: Yes for QMB and SLMB, but QI-1 recipients cannot have other Medicaid coverage. However, QI-1 automatically qualifies your parent for Extra Help with prescription drug costs.

faqs.question: What if my parent lives in NYC versus upstate — is the application different?
faqs.answer: The program rules are the same statewide, but NYC residents apply through HRA (888-692-6116) while other counties use their local Department of Social Services. Processing times and office procedures may vary slightly by location.

programs.name: Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP) / ESAP / NYSCAP
programs.tagline: Monthly grocery money for your parent if they're 60+ on a fixed income — potentially hundreds per month.

localEntities.name: Local Department of Social Services (DSS) offices county-wide

programs.intro:
If your parent is 60+ and living on Social Security or disability benefits, they may qualify for monthly grocery assistance through New York's enhanced SNAP programs. These programs put money on an EBT card your parent can use like a debit card at grocery stores and farmers markets.

New York offers two streamlined versions for seniors and disabled adults: ESAP (for households where all adults are 60+ or disabled with no earned income) and NYSCAP (which automatically enrolls SSI recipients living alone). Because these programs have simplified applications and 36-month certifications instead of frequent renewals, your parent faces less paperwork and fewer office visits than regular SNAP.

The monthly benefit amount depends on household size and income, calculated through SNAP's standard formula. Income limits are more generous in New York than federal minimums — households with elderly or disabled members can earn up to $2,608/month gross income for a single person.
programs.savingsSource: Monthly EBT benefits vary by household size and income

otherRequirements: All adult household members must be 60+ or receive disability benefits (SSI, SSD, VA 100% disability, or disability Medicaid)
No earned income from work (ESAP only)
Must live in New York State

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online at myBenefits.ny.gov or call your local DSS office — processing typically takes 30 days.

steps.title: Choose your application type
steps.description: Use form LDSS-5166 for ESAP if your parent is 60+ with no earned income, or the standard SNAP application online at myBenefits.ny.gov.

steps.title: Gather documents
steps.description: You'll need proof of income (Social Security award letters), ID, proof of address, and any disability verification letters.

steps.title: Submit application
steps.description: Apply online, call 1-800-342-3009, or visit your local Department of Social Services office.

steps.title: Wait for processing
steps.description: DSS will contact you if they need additional information. ESAP uses data matching to reduce verification requirements.

applicationGuide.processingTime: 30 days for standard applications, under 7 days if expedited for very low income households
applicationGuide.tip: If your parent receives SSI and lives alone, they may be automatically enrolled in NYSCAP without applying.

contentSections.heading: Income Limits by Household Size (ESAP)
contentSections.footnote: October 2025 - September 2026 limits for households with all adults 60+ or disabled

categories.name: Identity & Residence
items: Proof of New York address (utility bill, lease, mail)

contentSections.text: If your parent gets any earned income from work or a new adult moves into their household, they'll need to report this immediately — it could disqualify them from ESAP and require switching to regular SNAP.

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their house if they qualify for SNAP?
faqs.answer: Yes. The primary home where your parent lives doesn't count toward the $4,500 asset limit, regardless of its value.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent's Social Security income is just over the limit?
faqs.answer: Apply anyway — New York has more generous income limits than federal minimums, and the calculation includes deductions for rent, utilities, and medical expenses that could bring them under the threshold.

faqs.question: Does my parent need to go to interviews every few months?
faqs.answer: No. ESAP and NYSCAP have 36-month certification periods with no required recertification interviews, unlike regular SNAP which requires more frequent check-ins.

faqs.question: Can my parent use SNAP benefits to buy groceries online?
faqs.answer: Yes. The EBT card works for online grocery purchases at participating retailers like Amazon and Walmart, as well as in-store at grocery stores and farmers markets.

faqs.question: What if my parent receives SSI — do they still need to apply?
faqs.answer: If your parent receives SSI and lives alone, they may be automatically enrolled in NYSCAP without applying. If they live with others, they'll need to apply for ESAP.

faqs.question: What can't my parent buy with SNAP benefits?
faqs.answer: SNAP cannot be used for alcohol, tobacco, vitamins, hot prepared foods, pet food, or non-food items like soap or paper products. It's strictly for groceries you prepare at home.

programs.name: Home Energy Assistance Program (HEAP)
programs.tagline: If your parent pays their own heating or electric bills, they may qualify for $45–900+ toward energy costs each year.

localEntities.name: Local Department of Social Services (DSS) offices throughout New York State
HEAP Local District Contacts
National Grid (for Upstate NY customers)

programs.intro:
If your parent struggles with heating or electric bills in New York, HEAP provides direct payments to reduce these costs. The program pays $45–900+ per year depending on your parent's heating source and living situation — households using oil, propane, or kerosene can receive $900+ annually, while those in apartments with heating costs included in rent receive $45–50.

Your parent automatically qualifies if they receive SNAP, Temporary Assistance, or certain SSI benefits. Otherwise, their household income must be at or below 130% of the federal poverty level ($1,580/month for a single person in 2026). Because the heating or electric bill must be in your parent's name, tenants in government-subsidized housing where heat is included in rent cannot qualify.

HEAP operates year-round through local Department of Social Services offices, but Emergency HEAP (for shutoff situations) only runs January 2 through April 7, 2026.
programs.savingsSource: Base benefit amounts by heating source from NY OTDA

summary: Income at 130% federal poverty level or receives SNAP/TA/SSI
Must pay own heating costs (not included in rent)
otherRequirements: U.S. Citizen or Qualified Non-Citizen with valid Social Security number
Must reside in dwelling requesting assistance for
Cannot be in government-subsidized housing with heat included in rent

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online, by phone, or at your local Department of Social Services office — processing time not specified by the state.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Collect current utility bill in your parent's name, Social Security cards, photo ID, proof of income (pay stubs or pension statements), and proof of residence.

steps.title: Choose application method
steps.description: Apply online at ny.gov/services/apply-heating-assistance-heap, call 718-557-1399, or visit your local DSS office.

steps.title: Submit complete application
steps.description: Provide all required documentation. For Emergency HEAP (shutoffs), you must also include the disconnection notice and apply between January 2 – April 7, 2026.

applicationGuide.waitlist: Emergency HEAP opens January 2, 2026 and closes April 7, 2026
applicationGuide.tip: If your parent receives SNAP, they automatically qualify regardless of income — mention this when applying to speed up the process.

contentSections.heading: Income Limits by Household Size (130% Federal Poverty Level)
contentSections.footnote: 2026 Federal Poverty Guidelines. Automatic eligibility if receiving SNAP, TA, or Code A SSI.

tiers.name: Oil, Kerosene, or Propane Heat
tiers.description: Households paying directly to fuel vendor

tiers.name: Wood, Pellets, Coal Heat
tiers.description: Households paying directly for deliverable fuel

tiers.name: Apartment with Heat in Rent
tiers.description: Heating costs included in monthly rent payment

categories.name: Identity & Residence
items: Valid Social Security card for each household member
Photo ID (driver's license, passport, or state ID)
Proof of residency: water bill, tax bill, lease, or mortgage statement

categories.name: Utility Bills
items: Current heating or electric bill in applicant's name (required)
National Grid or other utility company statement
Disconnection notice (if applying for Emergency HEAP)

categories.name: Income Proof
items: Most recent 4 weeks of pay stubs
Note: Bank statements cannot be used for income verification

contentSections.text: Emergency HEAP for utility shutoffs only operates January 2 – April 7, 2026. If your parent receives a disconnection notice, apply immediately within this window.
The heating or electric bill must be in your parent's name — this is a hard requirement. If bills are in your name or a facility's name, they won't qualify.

faqs.question: What if Mom lives in senior housing where heat is included in her rent?
faqs.answer: If she lives in government-subsidized housing with heat included, she can only get $21/year. If she's in private senior housing but pays heating costs separately or they're added to her rent, she may qualify for $45-50/year.

faqs.question: Can Dad get HEAP if he already receives SNAP benefits?
faqs.answer: Yes — if your dad receives SNAP, Temporary Assistance, or Code A SSI, he automatically qualifies for HEAP regardless of his income level. This makes the application process faster.

faqs.question: What if the utility bill is in my name but Dad lives there and pays it?
faqs.answer: He won't qualify. The heating or electric bill must be in your dad's name. You'd need to contact the utility company to transfer the account to his name before applying.

faqs.question: How much can Mom get if she heats with oil?
faqs.answer: If your mom pays directly to the oil company and meets income limits, she can receive $900+ per year. Oil, kerosene, and propane users get the highest benefit amounts because these fuels cost more.

faqs.question: What counts as 'proof of income' if Dad is retired?
faqs.answer: Use his Social Security or pension statements. Bank statements don't count as income proof even if they show deposits. If he has investment income, bring dividend or interest statements.

faqs.question: Can we apply for emergency help if Mom's heat is about to be shut off?
faqs.answer: Yes, but only between January 2 – April 7, 2026. You'll need the disconnection notice from the utility company. Emergency HEAP has this limited window, so act quickly if you receive a shutoff notice during these months.

programs.name: State Health Insurance Assistance Program (SHIP) / Health Insurance Information, Counseling and Assistance Program (HIICAP)
programs.tagline: Get free, expert help navigating Medicare options and solving insurance problems — no income limits.

localEntities.name: Headquartered at NYS Office for the Aging, Two Empire State Plaza, Albany NY 12223; local via 59 AAAs

programs.intro:
If your parent is overwhelmed by Medicare choices or facing insurance problems, HIICAP provides free one-on-one counseling with trained experts. Because nearly 500 counselors statewide help with everything from choosing Medicare Advantage vs. Medigap to filing claims and finding low-income assistance programs, your parent gets unbiased guidance without sales pressure.

Available to all Medicare beneficiaries and New York residents approaching Medicare eligibility (typically age 65 or those under 65 with SSDI for 24 months). No income limits, asset tests, or fees — just free, confidential help understanding Medicare options, resolving billing issues, and connecting to programs like Extra Help or Medicare Savings Programs.

otherRequirements: Must be Medicare beneficiary or New York resident becoming Medicare eligible
U.S. citizens or legal permanent residents for at least 5 years (if not SSDI-eligible)

applicationGuide.summary: Call the hotline to be connected with your local counselor — counseling available immediately.

steps.title: Call the statewide hotline
steps.description: Dial 1-800-701-0501. Your call will be automatically routed to your county's HIICAP office based on your location.

steps.title: Schedule counseling session
steps.description: Speak with your local office to set up phone or in-person counseling. No forms required — just explain what Medicare help you need.

steps.title: Bring Medicare information
steps.description: Have your parent's Medicare card, current insurance details, and any billing problems ready for the most helpful session.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Immediate phone routing and counseling scheduling
applicationGuide.tip: NYC residents can also call 212-AGING-NYC (212-244-6469) for direct local connection.

contentSections.text: HIICAP only provides counseling and information — not direct financial aid or healthcare coverage. For income-based prescription help, ask your counselor about EPIC (income limits: $35,000 single/$50,000 couple).

faqs.question: What if my parent isn't on Medicare yet but is turning 65 soon?
faqs.answer: HIICAP helps New York residents who will become Medicare eligible, typically at age 65. Call 1-800-701-0501 to get guidance on enrollment timing, Medicare Advantage vs. Original Medicare + Medigap, and avoiding late enrollment penalties.

faqs.question: Can they help if Mom is already enrolled but having problems with Medicare billing or claims?
faqs.answer: Yes — HIICAP counselors help resolve Medicare billing issues, denied claims, and insurance problems. They can also help if your parent wants to switch Medicare plans during open enrollment.

faqs.question: Will the counselor try to sell my parent insurance?
faqs.answer: No. HIICAP counselors are trained volunteers who provide unbiased information only. They don't sell insurance or get paid by insurance companies — their job is to help your parent understand options without sales pressure.

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is too high for programs like Medicaid but they still need help with Medicare costs?
faqs.answer: HIICAP counselors can explain Medicare Savings Programs (which have higher income limits than Medicaid), Extra Help for prescription costs, and help compare Medicare Advantage vs. Medigap options to find the most affordable coverage.

faqs.question: Can I attend the counseling session with my parent?
faqs.answer: Yes, family members can participate in counseling sessions. HIICAP offers both phone and in-person sessions, so you can join to help your parent understand their options and take notes.

faqs.question: Is this the same as calling Medicare directly?
faqs.answer: No — Medicare's 1-800-MEDICARE provides basic information, but HIICAP counselors offer personalized, local guidance and can spend more time explaining complex situations. They also know New York-specific programs like EPIC that Medicare's national line might not mention.

programs.name: Meals on Wheels (via Expanded In-Home Services for the Elderly Program - EISEP)
programs.tagline: If your parent needs help with daily tasks, they may qualify for home-delivered meals 5 days a week plus other support services.

localEntities.name: Local Area Agencies on Aging; examples: Wyoming County (585-786-8833), Genesee County (585-343-1611), Nassau via EAC Network, Monroe via Catholic Charities/FCS

programs.intro:
If your parent is 60+ and struggles with daily activities like cooking or bathing, New York's EISEP program provides home-delivered meals (Meals on Wheels) plus other support services to help them stay home safely. Because EISEP covers multiple services under one case manager, your parent gets coordinated care instead of managing separate programs.

The program delivers hot meals 5 days a week, typically around lunch time. Your parent may also qualify for housekeeping, personal care, or emergency response systems. There are no strict income limits — anyone 60+ can apply — but families pay sliding-scale fees starting around $4 per meal if income exceeds 150% of the poverty level ($2,235/month for one person in 2026).
programs.savingsSource: Based on suggested contribution of ~$4/meal for 5 meals/week (52 weeks)

summary: Can be maintained safely at home
Not eligible for same services through Medicaid
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must need assistance with at least one activity of daily living (bathing, dressing, eating) or two instrumental activities of daily living (cooking, housekeeping, medication management). For meals specifically, must be physically or mentally unable to prepare nutritious meals.
otherRequirements: Must be able to live safely at home with services
Cannot be eligible for the same services under Medicaid
Must be physically or mentally incapacitated and unable to prepare nutritious meals (for meal delivery)

applicationGuide.summary: Call your local Area Agency on Aging for a comprehensive assessment — no forms to fill out upfront.

steps.title: Contact your local Area Agency on Aging
steps.description: Call 1-800-342-9871 for NY Connects or find your county-specific office. In NYC, enter your address at citymeals.org/get-meals to find your case management agency.

steps.title: Schedule a comprehensive assessment
steps.description: A case manager will assess your parent's functional needs, living situation, and ability to be maintained safely at home.

steps.title: Provide income information
steps.description: The case manager will determine cost-sharing fees based on income. Prepare details about all income sources.

steps.title: Receive service coordination
steps.description: If eligible, the case manager coordinates meals delivery and any additional services like housekeeping or personal care.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Assessment scheduled after initial contact — timeline varies by county
applicationGuide.tip: Even if your parent receives some Medicaid services, they may still qualify for EISEP meals if their dietary needs aren't being met.

offices.name: Wyoming County Office for the Aging
Genesee County Office for the Aging

contentSections.text: Meals on Wheels is just one part of EISEP — you cannot apply for meals alone. Your parent must go through the full EISEP eligibility assessment and case management process.

Even though the program accepts applicants regardless of income, families with income above 150% of poverty level (about $2,235/month) will pay sliding-scale fees. No one is refused service for inability to pay.

faqs.question: What if my parent already gets some Medicaid services — can they still get meals through EISEP?
faqs.answer: Yes, if Medicaid isn't covering meals or if their current meal services don't meet their dietary needs. The program excludes people only when Medicaid already covers the exact same services.

faqs.question: How much will my parent pay for meals if their income is above the poverty level?
faqs.answer: Fees start around $4 per meal if income exceeds 150% of poverty level ($2,235/month for one person in 2026). The exact amount depends on total income and which services they receive. No one is refused service for inability to pay.

faqs.question: Can my parent get just the meals, or do they have to accept other services too?
faqs.answer: Your parent must go through the full EISEP assessment and case management, but they can decline specific services they don't want. Case management is required for everyone, but additional services depend on needs and preferences.

faqs.question: What days and times are meals delivered?
faqs.answer: Typically 5 days per week during lunch hours (around 11am-1:30pm), but exact timing varies by county and local provider. Your case manager will provide specific delivery details for your area.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent's needs change after they start receiving services?
faqs.answer: The case manager reassesses needs periodically and can add or adjust services. Because everything is coordinated through EISEP, changes can be made without applying to separate programs.

faqs.question: Does my parent need to be homebound to qualify for meal delivery?
faqs.answer: No, they don't need to be homebound, but they must be physically or mentally unable to prepare nutritious meals and have no one else available to do so. The assessment focuses on functional ability, not mobility restrictions.

programs.name: Respite Care Program (NYFSC) / Caregiver Resource Centers
programs.tagline: Get trained home health aide coverage for your NYC parent so you can take breaks from daily caregiving.

localEntities.name: Single intake office: 11 Park Place, 14th Floor, New York, NY 10007-2801

programs.intro:
If you're caring for a parent over 60 in New York City who isn't eligible for Medicaid, NYFSC provides trained home health aides to give you respite breaks. Your parent pays starting at $7.15/hour plus carfare for basic services like personal care, light housekeeping, shopping, and companionship.

Because this program specifically serves non-Medicaid eligible seniors, it fills a gap for families who earn too much for Medicaid but still need affordable care support. Emergency and extended hours services are free for families with annual incomes of $40,000 or less. The program operates throughout all five NYC boroughs but requires your parent to live in the city and have a daily unpaid caregiver (you) providing regular assistance.

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Your parent must be frail or have disabilities requiring daily assistance or regular supervision
otherRequirements: Must live in New York City (all five boroughs)
Must NOT be eligible for Medicaid
Must have unpaid family caregiver providing daily care
Caregiver must be 18 or older

applicationGuide.summary: Call (212) 962-7559 to start intake — a social worker will schedule a home visit to assess your parent's care needs.

steps.title: Contact NYFSC
steps.description: Call (212) 962-7559 or email nyfscinc@aol.com to request an intake form and schedule assessment.

steps.title: Complete intake form
steps.description: Fill out the intake form with your parent's information and care needs.

steps.title: Home assessment
steps.description: NYFSC social worker visits your parent's home to evaluate need for respite care services.

steps.title: Service matching
steps.description: If approved, NYFSC matches your family with trained home health aides based on schedule and care needs.

applicationGuide.tip: Gather proof that your parent is NOT Medicaid eligible before the home visit — this is required for the program.

tiers.name: Basic Respite Care
tiers.description: Personal care, light housekeeping, shopping, cooking, laundry, escorting, companionship
tiers.coverage: Starting at $7.15/hour plus carfare (your parent pays directly)

tiers.name: Emergency/Extended Hours
tiers.description: Same services during emergency situations or extended hours

offices.name: NYFSC Intake Office
offices.address: 11 Park Place, 14th Floor, New York, NY 10007-2801

categories.name: Identity & Residence
items: Proof your parent is over 60 (birth certificate, ID)
Documentation of NYC residency (lease, utility bill)

categories.name: Medical & Care Status
items: Documentation that your parent is NOT Medicaid eligible
Evidence of functional impairment requiring daily care/supervision
List of current medications and medical conditions

contentSections.text: This program only serves NYC residents. If your parent lives anywhere else in New York State, contact your local Area Agency on Aging instead.
If your parent qualifies for Medicaid, they're not eligible for this program — but they may get better coverage through NY state Medicaid waiver programs.

faqs.question: What if my parent just became eligible for Medicaid?
faqs.answer: They would no longer qualify for NYFSC respite care. Contact NY Office for the Aging at (800) 342-9871 to explore Medicaid waiver programs that provide similar services.

faqs.question: How much will this actually cost per month?
faqs.answer: It depends on hours needed. At $7.15/hour plus carfare, 10 hours per week would cost roughly $312/month. Emergency services are free if your family's annual income is $40,000 or less.

faqs.question: Can I use this program if I work full-time?
faqs.answer: Yes, but you still need to be providing daily unpaid care when you're home. The program is designed to give working caregivers respite breaks, not replace full-time care.

faqs.question: What if my parent lives in Queens but I live in New Jersey?
faqs.answer: That's fine. Your parent must live in NYC (any borough), but you as the caregiver can live anywhere as long as you're 18+ and providing daily care.

faqs.question: How do I know if my parent is too wealthy for Medicaid but could still afford this?
faqs.answer: Medicaid eligibility is complex. Contact NYFSC at (212) 962-7559 — they can help determine if your parent falls in the gap between Medicaid and private pay.

faqs.question: What's the difference between this and hiring a home health aide privately?
faqs.answer: NYFSC aides are trained and certified through the program, rates may be lower than private agencies, and you get support from social workers for care coordination.

programs.name: Senior Community Service Employment Program (SCSEP)
programs.tagline: Part-time paid work and job training for unemployed parents 55+ with low income

localEntities.name: NYC: Aging Connect (212-244-6469) for referral
Westchester County: Senior Citizens Department
York County: Crispus Attucks York (Monday–Friday, 8:30 AM–5:00 PM)
CPC (Community Preservation Corporation) in NYC: Penny Shen, pshen@cpc-nyc.org
Approximately 50 local partner organizations statewide through CWI Works

programs.intro:
If your parent is 55+ and unemployed with low income, SCSEP provides part-time paid work at nonprofit organizations while helping them find permanent employment. Your parent works an average of 20 hours per week at minimum wage ($15+/hour in New York, varying by locality) — approximately $1,200–$1,600+ per month.

Because SCSEP combines paid work with job training and placement assistance, your parent earns income immediately while building skills for permanent employment. The program places participants at community organizations like senior centers, schools, and hospitals where they gain work experience and receive training in computer skills, customer service, or other in-demand fields.

Your parent must be unemployed and have family income at or below 125% of the federal poverty level (approximately $1,548/month for a single person in 2026). Veterans, people 65+, and those with disabilities get priority enrollment.
programs.savingsSource: 20 hours/week at New York minimum wage ($15-$20/hour depending on locality)

otherRequirements: Must be currently unemployed
Must be able to work 20 hours per week

applicationGuide.summary: Apply in person at your local SCSEP office or call NYC residents can call 212-244-6469 for referral — processing time varies by location.

steps.title: Contact your local SCSEP office
steps.description: NYC residents call Aging Connect at 212-244-6469. Other areas contact your county's SCSEP provider or local American Job Center for referral.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Bring state/federal photo ID, Social Security card, proof of all income sources, and family income documentation.

steps.title: Complete application interview
steps.description: Staff will assess eligibility, create an Individual Employment Plan, and discuss available community service placements in your area.

steps.title: Begin orientation and training
steps.description: Once accepted, complete program orientation and job-specific training before starting your community service assignment.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Varies by location and funding availability
applicationGuide.waitlist: Program is experiencing federal funding delays which may affect availability
applicationGuide.tip: Veterans, people 65+, and those with disabilities get priority — mention these qualifications when applying.

contentSections.heading: Income Limits by Household Size (125% Federal Poverty Level)
contentSections.footnote: 2026 federal poverty guidelines. Includes ALL income: Social Security, pensions, part-time work.

categories.name: Financial
items: Tax returns or pay stubs for all family income

contentSections.text: The program is experiencing federal funding delays. Availability and start dates may vary by region. Contact your local office for current status.
This is temporary training work, not permanent employment. The goal is to help your parent find unsubsidized employment after 6-24 months in the program.

faqs.question: What if my parent has Social Security income — does that count toward the income limit?
faqs.answer: Yes, ALL income counts including Social Security, pensions, and any part-time work. Family income must be below 125% of federal poverty level ($1,548/month for a single person in 2026).

faqs.question: Can my parent choose where they work or what type of job training they get?
faqs.answer: SCSEP places participants at nonprofit organizations, schools, senior centers, and government agencies based on available openings. Training varies by location — examples include computer skills, customer service, home health aide, and clerical work.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent finds a regular job while in SCSEP?
faqs.answer: That's the goal. SCSEP provides job placement assistance and helps participants transition to unsubsidized employment. Your parent leaves the program once they find permanent work.

faqs.question: How long can my parent stay in the program?
faqs.answer: SCSEP is designed as temporary training, typically 6-24 months. The program focuses on building skills and work history to help participants find permanent employment.

faqs.question: Does my parent's house or savings affect eligibility?
faqs.answer: The program information doesn't specify asset limits. Contact your local SCSEP office to clarify whether savings, home equity, or other assets affect eligibility.

faqs.question: What if my parent doesn't fall into a priority group — can they still get in?
faqs.answer: Yes, but veterans, people 65+, those with disabilities, and other priority groups get enrolled first. During funding shortages, non-priority applicants may wait longer or face limited availability.

programs.name: Senior Citizen Homeowners' Exemption (SCHE)
programs.tagline: If your parent owns their NYC home and has income under $58,399, they could save hundreds to thousands on property taxes through a 5-50% tax reduction.

localEntities.name: NYC Department of Finance

programs.intro:
If your parent is 65+ and owns their home in New York City, they may qualify for significant property tax savings through the Senior Citizen Homeowners' Exemption (SCHE). This program reduces the assessed value of their property by 5% to 50%, depending on their income level. For example, a parent with income under $50,000 gets a 50% reduction in assessed value, while those earning $57,500-$58,399 still get a 5% reduction.

The program applies to one-, two-, or three-family homes, condos, and co-ops across all five NYC boroughs. Because the exemption reduces the assessed value used to calculate property taxes, your parent's actual savings depend on their property's value and tax rate. Your parent must have owned and lived in the property as their primary residence for at least 12 consecutive months before applying.
programs.savingsSource: Property tax reduction based on assessed value

summary: Own and live in NYC property
otherRequirements: Must own property for 12+ consecutive months
Property must be primary residence
Applies to 1-3 family homes, condos, or co-ops only

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online through NYC Department of Finance website between September 15 and March 15 annually.

steps.title: Gather income documentation
steps.description: Collect tax returns, Social Security statements, retirement benefit records, and any IRA distribution documents for all property owners and spouses.

steps.title: Calculate combined household income
steps.description: Add all income sources including Social Security, retirement benefits, interest, dividends, wages, and capital gains for all owners and spouses.

steps.title: Submit online application
steps.description: Complete the SCHE online initial application at NYC Department of Finance website between September 15 and March 15.

steps.title: Provide proof of ownership and residency
steps.description: Upload documentation showing 12+ months of property ownership and that the property is the primary residence.

applicationGuide.tip: Don't forget to include your spouse's income even if they're not on the property deed — it still counts toward the income limit.

categories.name: Income Verification
items: Tax returns (if filed) or income estimate documentation
Documentation of unreimbursed medical expenses (if deducting)

categories.name: Property Documentation
items: Proof of property ownership for 12+ consecutive months
Documentation showing property as primary residence

contentSections.text: Applications are only accepted September 15 through March 15 each year. Miss this window and you'll wait until the following year to apply.

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is just over $58,399?
faqs.answer: Unfortunately, there's no exemption available if combined household income exceeds $58,399. However, if your parent's income fluctuates year to year, they can reapply when their income drops below the limit.

faqs.question: Does my parent's spouse's income count even if the spouse isn't on the deed?
faqs.answer: Yes, spouse income must be included in the total calculation unless they are legally separated or one spouse has abandoned the other. This is true even if the spouse doesn't own the property.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep this exemption if she moves to a different house in NYC?
faqs.answer: She'll need to reapply for the new property and meet the 12-month ownership requirement, unless she was already receiving SCHE on her previous residence — in that case, the 12-month requirement is waived.

faqs.question: What counts as income for this program?
faqs.answer: All income sources count: Social Security, pensions, retirement account distributions, interest, dividends, capital gains, wages, rental income, and self-employment income. Use your adjusted gross income from tax returns minus taxable IRA distributions.

faqs.question: Does this program exist outside of New York City?
faqs.answer: No, SCHE is NYC-specific. Other New York municipalities may offer their own Senior Citizens Exemption programs with different income limits and rules set by each locality.

faqs.question: What if part of the house is used for business?
faqs.answer: The exemption only applies to the residential portion of the property. If your parent runs a business from part of the home, that portion won't qualify for the tax reduction.

programs.name: Senior Citizen Rent Increase Exemption (SCRIE)
programs.tagline: Freeze your parent's rent permanently if they're 62+, pay more than a third of income for rent, and live in regulated housing.

localEntities.name: NYC Department of Finance (citywide)
HPD for HPD developments

programs.intro:
If your parent is 62 or older and struggling with rising rent costs in New York City, SCRIE can freeze their rent at the current level forever. This program stops all future legal rent increases for seniors who spend at least one-third of their income on rent and earn $50,000 or less per year.

The key requirement: your parent must live in rent-regulated housing (rent-stabilized, rent-controlled, Mitchell-Lama, or certain cooperatives). Most market-rate apartments don't qualify. Because SCRIE provides a tax credit to landlords to cover the frozen increases, your parent's rent stays locked while the landlord receives compensation from the city.

This isn't automatic — your parent must apply and renew the benefit every time they get a new lease or when notified by the city, typically every few years.

otherRequirements: Annual household income must be $50,000 or less
Must pay at least one-third of household income toward rent
Must live in rent-regulated housing (rent-stabilized, rent-controlled, Mitchell-Lama, limited dividend, redevelopment companies, Section 213 cooperatives, or HDFC cooperatives)
Must be named on the lease or rent order

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online through NYC's Tenant Access Portal — processing time not specified by the city.

steps.title: Confirm your parent's apartment qualifies
steps.description: SCRIE only covers rent-regulated apartments. Check the lease for terms like 'rent-stabilized' or 'rent-controlled,' or look up the building on NYC's Housing Portal.

steps.title: Gather income documentation
steps.description: Collect proof of income for everyone in the household from the prior calendar year — Social Security statements, pension records, bank statements.

steps.title: Get rent documents
steps.description: You'll need the current lease, any rent increase notices, and details about current monthly rent payments.

steps.title: Submit SCRIE application online
steps.description: Use NYC's Tenant Access Portal at the Rent Freeze Program page. Phone and mail options available by calling 311.

applicationGuide.tip: If your parent's income went over $50,000 one year but dropped back down, they can reapply — a temporary income spike doesn't permanently disqualify them.

offices.name: HPD Developments
offices.address: For residents of HPD-supervised developments only

contentSections.text: You must renew SCRIE with every new lease or when the city sends a renewal notice (usually every few years). Missing renewal means losing the rent freeze permanently.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent's income goes over $50,000 one year?
faqs.answer: They'll lose SCRIE benefits for that year, but can reapply the following calendar year if their income drops back below $50,000. A temporary income spike doesn't permanently disqualify them.

faqs.question: How do I know if my parent's apartment qualifies for SCRIE?
faqs.answer: Look for terms like 'rent-stabilized' or 'rent-controlled' on the lease, or check if it's Mitchell-Lama housing. Market-rate apartments don't qualify. You can verify on NYC's Housing Portal or call 311.

faqs.question: Do unemployment benefits count toward the $50,000 income limit?
faqs.answer: Yes, pandemic unemployment benefits count as income. However, federal stimulus checks do not count toward the income limit.

faqs.question: What if my parent forgets to renew their SCRIE benefits?
faqs.answer: They'll lose the rent freeze and have to pay all increases. There's no automatic renewal — they must respond to renewal notices or apply with each new lease.

faqs.question: Does my parent's savings account affect SCRIE eligibility?
faqs.answer: No, SCRIE has no asset limits. Only income matters — your parent can have savings, investments, or other assets without affecting eligibility.

faqs.question: Can my parent get SCRIE if they live outside New York City?
faqs.answer: SCRIE is primarily a NYC program, but some municipalities in Nassau and Westchester counties offer it. Contact your local housing authority to check availability.

programs.name: Enhanced STAR Program
programs.tagline: If your parent is 65+ and owns their New York home, they can get enhanced property tax savings on school taxes.
programs.intro: If your parent is 65+ and owns their home in New York, Enhanced STAR can reduce their school property taxes more than Basic STAR. Because this program provides a tax exemption directly on school taxes, your parent keeps more money in their pocket each year without changing how they file taxes. The program covers all New York homeowners who meet age and income requirements. Combined household income must be below $110,750 (2026 limit based on 2024 income). Starting in 2026, if your parent already has Basic STAR and becomes eligible for Enhanced STAR, New York State will automatically upgrade them — no separate application needed.
programs.savingsSource: Property tax exemption amount varies by local school tax rates

summary: Own and live in the home
otherRequirements: Must own the property as primary residence
Must enroll in Income Verification Program (except new homeowners or first-time STAR applicants)

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online at tax.ny.gov/star or call (518) 457-2036 — processing handled by New York State starting in 2026.

steps.title: Check if you need to apply
steps.description: If your parent already has Basic STAR and turns 65, New York State will automatically upgrade them starting in 2026. New applicants still need to apply.

steps.title: Gather income documents
steps.description: Get your parent's 2024 tax return (pages 1 and 2) if applying for 2026 Enhanced STAR. Income from 2 years prior determines eligibility.

steps.title: Submit application before March 1
steps.description: Apply online at tax.ny.gov/star, call (518) 457-2036, or through your local assessor's office. Deadline varies by town but typically March 1.

steps.title: Complete Income Verification Program enrollment
steps.description: Required for Enhanced STAR applicants (except new homeowners or first-time applicants). You only enroll once — New York State verifies income annually after that.

applicationGuide.tip: Your parent can apply in the year they turn 65, even if their 65th birthday is December 31st, as long as they apply before the March 1 deadline.

categories.name: Identity
items: Proof of age: driver's license, birth certificate, or passport copy

categories.name: Financial
items: Federal or New York State tax return from 2 years prior (pages 1 and 2) — for 2026 Enhanced STAR, provide 2024 tax return
Income worksheet if not required to file annual NYS Income Tax Return

categories.name: Property
items: Most recent school tax bill (if available)

contentSections.text: The $110,750 income limit includes ALL property owners (even those who don't live there) plus any owner's spouse who lives at the property. Make sure to count everyone's income when determining eligibility.

Application deadline is typically March 1 but varies by town. If you miss the deadline, you may still qualify if you can demonstrate good cause, but this requires additional steps with your local assessor.

faqs.question: My mom already gets Basic STAR and just turned 65. Does she need to apply separately for Enhanced STAR?
faqs.answer: Starting in 2026, no separate application needed. If she's income-eligible for Enhanced STAR, New York State will automatically notify her local assessor to upgrade her from Basic to Enhanced STAR.

faqs.question: My dad owns the house but my stepmom also lives there. Whose income counts toward the $110,750 limit?
faqs.answer: Both incomes count. The limit includes all property owners (residents and non-residents) plus any owner's spouse who lives at the property. If their combined income exceeds $110,750, they don't qualify.

faqs.question: Mom's income was $109,000 in 2024 but will be higher in 2026. Which year determines her eligibility?
faqs.answer: Her 2024 income determines 2026 Enhanced STAR eligibility. The program uses income from 2 years prior, so 2026 benefits are based on 2024 tax returns.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent's property has multiple owners who don't all live there?
faqs.answer: All owners' income counts toward the $110,750 limit, even non-resident owners. However, only one resident owner needs to be 65+ for age qualification if they're spouses or siblings.

faqs.question: How much money will Enhanced STAR actually save on my parent's tax bill?
faqs.answer: Savings depend on your local school tax rates. Enhanced STAR provides greater savings than Basic STAR, and the tax savings increase by 2% yearly, but the exact dollar amount varies by school district.

faqs.question: My parent missed the March 1 deadline. Can they still apply?
faqs.answer: Possibly. They can still apply if they can demonstrate 'good cause' for missing the deadline, but this requires additional action and approval from the local assessor. Contact your local assessor's office immediately.

programs.name: Expanded In-home Services for the Elderly Program (EISEP)
programs.tagline: Get help with bathing, cooking, and housekeeping for your parent who doesn't qualify for Medicaid

localEntities.name: Local Area Agencies on Aging
NY Connects
Examples: EAC Network (Nassau), Jefferson County Office for the Aging, Catholic Charities/Family and Community Services (Monroe)

programs.intro:
If your parent needs help with daily tasks like bathing, cooking, or housekeeping but doesn't qualify for Medicaid, EISEP provides in-home assistance on a sliding scale based on their income. Your parent gets a case manager who coordinates all services, plus home care aides for personal care (bathing, dressing, grooming) and household tasks (cleaning, shopping, meal prep).

EISEP also offers respite care to give family caregivers a break, home-delivered meals, emergency response systems, and subsidized adult day care. The program is designed to supplement the care you're already providing — not replace it. Because services are based on a comprehensive assessment, your parent gets exactly what they need rather than a one-size-fits-all package.
programs.savingsSource: Sliding scale service based on income

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must need assistance with personal care or household chores due to illness or disability
otherRequirements: Cannot be eligible for Medicaid
Must have existing informal care that EISEP will supplement

applicationGuide.summary: Call your local Area Agency on Aging or NY Connects at 1-800-342-9871 for assessment scheduling.

steps.title: Call your local Area Agency on Aging
steps.description: Use NY Connects at 1-800-342-9871 to find your local office, or contact them directly if you know your county office

steps.title: Schedule comprehensive assessment
steps.description: A case manager will evaluate your parent's needs for personal care, household tasks, and safety

steps.title: Receive service plan
steps.description: Based on assessment, you'll get a plan for case management plus additional services like home care aides or meal delivery

steps.title: Determine cost-share
steps.description: Your parent's contribution is calculated on a sliding scale from no-cost to full-cost based on income and services needed

applicationGuide.processingTime: Depends on home care aide availability in your area
applicationGuide.waitlist: Wait times vary by region based on local provider and aide availability
applicationGuide.tip: All participants receive case management first — additional services are added based on your parent's specific needs assessment.

tiers.name: Core Services (All Participants)
tiers.description: Case management including assessment, service coordination, and ongoing monitoring

tiers.name: Personal Care I
tiers.incomeLimit: Based on assessment and local availability

tiers.name: Personal Care II
tiers.incomeLimit: Based on assessment and local availability

tiers.name: Ancillary Services
tiers.description: Home-delivered meals, emergency response systems, respite care, subsidized adult day care, home repairs
tiers.incomeLimit: Based on assessment and local availability

offices.name: NY Connects (Statewide)
offices.address: Connects you to local Area Agency on Aging

offices.name: Jefferson County Office for the Aging
offices.address: Application available online or by phone

offices.name: Monroe County - Multiple Providers
offices.phone: Contact through Catholic Charities, Urban League of Rochester, Lifespan, or Jewish Family Service

categories.name: Income Information
items: Recent pay stubs, Social Security statements, or pension documents for sliding scale calculation
Bank statements to show current financial situation

categories.name: Health Information
items: List of current medications and medical conditions
Doctor's notes about need for assistance with daily activities

contentSections.text: Wait times for home care aides vary significantly by region. Some areas have immediate availability while others may have months-long waits. Ask your local office about current wait times when you apply.

EISEP supplements the care you're already providing — it doesn't replace family caregiving. If your parent becomes eligible for Medicaid later, they'll need to transition off EISEP to Medicaid home care services.

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is too high for other programs but they still can't afford full-price home care?
faqs.answer: EISEP has no strict income limits and uses a sliding scale. Even higher-income seniors may qualify for reduced-cost services. The program is specifically designed for people who don't qualify for Medicaid but still need financial help with care.

faqs.question: Can my parent get EISEP services if I'm already helping with some care?
faqs.answer: Yes, EISEP is designed to supplement existing family care, not replace it. The case manager will assess what additional help your parent needs beyond what family members are providing.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent becomes eligible for Medicaid while receiving EISEP services?
faqs.answer: Your parent will need to transition off EISEP since the program is specifically for people not eligible for Medicaid. However, they can then apply for Medicaid home care services, which may provide more comprehensive coverage.

faqs.question: How many hours of home care aide services can my parent get through EISEP?
faqs.answer: There are no fixed hour limits. Services are based on your parent's assessed needs and what's available through local providers. The case manager will work with you to determine the right amount of care.

faqs.question: Does my parent have to pay anything upfront, or can they be billed later?
faqs.answer: Cost-sharing arrangements vary by local office. Some may require upfront payment while others bill after services. Ask your local Area Agency on Aging about their specific payment procedures during the assessment.

faqs.question: What if there are no home care aides available in our area?
faqs.answer: Availability varies significantly by region. If aides aren't available immediately, you'll be placed on a waiting list. In the meantime, your parent will still receive case management services and may be eligible for other EISEP services like meal delivery or emergency response systems.

programs.name: Golden Park Program
programs.tagline: Free weekday access to New York state parks for your parent age 62+
programs.intro:
If your parent is 62+ and lives in New York, they get free vehicle access to most state parks, boat launches, and arboretums Monday through Friday. No income limits, no application, no waiting period — just show their New York driver's license or state ID at the entrance.

Because this covers vehicle entry fees at dozens of state parks, your parent can enjoy outdoor activities without worrying about daily admission costs. The program also includes fee reductions at state historic sites and golf courses operated by New York State Parks.

summary: Valid NY driver's license or state ID
otherRequirements: New York State resident
Current valid New York State Driver's License or Non-Driver Identification Card

applicationGuide.summary: No application needed — your parent shows their New York ID at any participating park entrance for immediate access.

steps.title: Bring valid ID
steps.description: Your parent needs their current New York driver's license or state-issued non-driver ID

steps.title: Visit weekdays only
steps.description: Free access is Monday-Friday, excluding holidays — weekend visits require regular fees

steps.title: Present ID at entrance
steps.description: Show ID to park attendant for free vehicle entry; for automated stations without staff, call the facility directly first

applicationGuide.tip: Call ahead to automated pay stations without attendants to confirm the entry process before driving out.

contentSections.text: Free access is weekdays only (Monday-Friday, excluding holidays). Your parent pays regular fees on weekends and holidays at all parks.

faqs.question: Which parks don't accept the Golden Park Program?
faqs.answer: The program is not valid at Walkway Over the Hudson, Earl W. Brydges Artpark, Lake Superior State Park, Fahnestock Winter Park, Mark Twain State Park, Soaring Eagles Golf Course, and Ganondagan State Historic Site. Your parent pays regular fees at these locations.

faqs.question: Can my parent use this at national parks like Niagara Falls?
faqs.answer: No, this only covers New York State parks. For national parks, your parent needs the separate America the Beautiful Senior Pass, which costs $80 for lifetime access to all national parks.

faqs.question: What if the park entrance has an automated pay station with no attendant?
faqs.answer: Call the facility directly before visiting. Some automated stations may not have a process for Golden Park access, and calling ahead prevents your parent from being stuck at an entrance gate.

faqs.question: Does this cover my parent's guests or just their vehicle?
faqs.answer: The program covers vehicle access, so everyone in your parent's car gets free entry on weekdays. However, some parks may charge separate fees for activities like camping, boat rentals, or special events.

faqs.question: My parent has an out-of-state driver's license but lives in New York. Do they qualify?
faqs.answer: No, they need a current New York State driver's license or New York non-driver ID card. Being a resident isn't enough — the ID must be issued by New York State.

faqs.question: What's included in the fee reductions at historic sites and golf courses?
faqs.answer: Your parent gets discounted admission at state historic sites and reduced greens fees at state-operated golf courses, but the specific discount amounts vary by location. Call the facility directly for current rates.

stateOverview.intro: New York offers 14 programs that can save your aging parent hundreds to thousands of dollars per year — from $100-300 monthly grocery money through SNAP to permanent rent freezes through SCRIE to property tax reductions of up to 50%. These range from income-based benefits (like Community Medicaid for home care and PACE for comprehensive medical support) to free resources available to all seniors (like expert Medicare help through HIICAP and free state park access). If your parent is struggling with rising costs or needs daily support, multiple programs may apply — many seniors qualify for several simultaneously. Start by checking the most impactful programs first, then work through others that match your parent's specific needs.

startHere.name: Community Medicaid
startHere.why: Covers home care services for parents who need daily help but want to avoid nursing homes — potentially worth thousands monthly.

startHere.name: Medicare Savings Program (MSP)
startHere.why: Helps pay Medicare costs if your parent's income is below $1,715/month — savings most seniors can use immediately.

startHere.name: SNAP
startHere.why: Provides $100-300+ monthly for groceries if your parent is 60+ on fixed income — quick application with fast results.

byNeed.need: Help with daily care at home
byNeed.description: Programs that provide home care, medical coordination, meal delivery, and daily support services.
Coverage for Medicare premiums, deductibles, and comprehensive medical care.
Monthly grocery money and home-delivered meals for seniors who need nutrition support.
Rent freezes, property tax reductions, and help with heating and electric bills.
Free resources including Medicare guidance, temporary care coverage, and recreation access.
Part-time paid work and job training for unemployed parents 55+ with low income.

quickFacts: Most New York benefit programs use income limits around $1,700-2,200/month for single seniors, but some (like Enhanced STAR) go higher
New York City has specific programs (SCRIE, SCHE, NYC Respite Care) that aren't available in other parts of the state
HIICAP provides free Medicare help with no income requirements — available statewide through local offices
stateOverview.resourcesVsBenefits: New York offers both income-based benefits that require qualification (like Community Medicaid for home care or SNAP for groceries) and free resources available to all seniors regardless of income (like HIICAP for Medicare help and Golden Park Program for free state park access). Start with free resources since there's no application process, then pursue benefits based on your parent's income and needs.
